title: The Books Just Closed for EOFY. The Half-Year Conversation Hasn't Started.
linkedin:
Your FY26 books just closed. So when does anyone in the business actually sit down and ask what those numbers mean for the next six months?

For most owner-led businesses, the honest answer is never. The EOFY figures get filed, dusted off again next year when the tax return needs them, and otherwise left alone.

Here is the asymmetry we see constantly across owner-led businesses. The most accurate, most detailed financial picture of the entire year lands in July, right at the point when almost nobody is looking at it. By August, everyone is back in the day to day, and the half-year debrief that should have happened quietly never does.

This is not a compliance gap. Your bookkeeper or BAS agent has done exactly what they are meant to do, reconciling the year and lodging on time. That is real, necessary work, and it is a different job entirely to asking what the numbers say about pricing, hiring or cash for the second half.

Done properly, a half-year debrief does not need to be a big exercise. Three specific risks the second half is carrying. Three specific opportunities worth chasing while there is still time. That clarity does more for a business than another dense report nobody reads past the first page.

The reason this rarely happens on its own is not a lack of discipline. It is that nobody inside the business is specifically responsible for stepping back and running it, and the owner, the one person with the full picture, is also the busiest person in the building.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#FractionalCFO #BusinessOwners #FinancialStrategy
facebook:
FY26 is closed. Quick question for business owners: has anyone actually sat down yet and asked what those numbers mean for the next six months?

Most of the time the answer is no, not because owners do not care, but because nobody in the business is specifically responsible for stepping back and doing it. The EOFY numbers are the most accurate read you will get all year, and July is often the exact moment nobody is looking at them.

Have you built any kind of mid-year check-in into your business, or does it tend to slide until next EOFY rolls around?

#SMEFinance
google:
A note for East Coast business owners this week, particularly if FY26 has just been finalised with your accountant.

Most owner-led businesses treat EOFY purely as a compliance milestone. The BAS is lodged, the tax position is settled, and the numbers get filed away until next year's return needs them. What often gets missed is that those same numbers are the most accurate financial picture the business will have all year, and July is usually the exact point when nobody is looking at them.

A proper half-year check-in does not need to be complicated. It asks where the first six months landed against plan, what changed that the plan did not anticipate, and what needs to shift for the rest of the year on pricing, hiring or cash. Three clear risks and three clear opportunities, named plainly, tend to do more good than a lengthy report nobody reads twice.

The reason this conversation often does not happen on its own is simply that nobody inside the business is specifically responsible for making time for it. We work with owner-led businesses across Brisbane, Queensland, New South Wales and Victoria on exactly this kind of half-year review.

Read the full piece on the blog.
